generated_summary: – Two days after the mass shooting at a Pittsburgh synagogue, a Republican candidate for a state Senate seat in Connecticut sent out a mailer that's being called anti-Semitic, the Washington Post reports. According to the Hartford Courant, the mailer shows Democratic state Rep. Matt Lesser "with large, beady eyes, holding fistfuls of hundred-dollar bills." On the other side, it reads, "Matt Lesser will take everything you worked for." The mailer was sent out by GOP candidate Ed Charamut. Lesser says he was shocked when he first saw it. “It uses imagery that’s been used to vilify the Jewish people for hundreds of years, and grossly caricatured my face and has me clutching a pile of money,” he tells the Courant. "That's as explicitly anti-Semitism as anything can be.” Lesser's Jewish campaign treasurer, Allan Greenspan, says the image of Lesser was altered to make him look "bigger and greedy." Greenspan says the money was also added to the picture. The Anti-Defamation League calls it an "age-oldAnti-Semitic trope." And state Republican Party Chair JR Romano calls it "false outrage." Lesser blames President Trump for allowing "racist, homophobes, and anti-Semites to drop their dog whistles and express their bigotry clearly and openly." He says he's "shaking my head as to the lengths some people will go to get elected or to stay in office, particularly in the present climate."

– Six Greenpeace activists have scaled the 87-story "Sh Shard" skyscraper in London to protest Shell's plans to drill in the Arctic, the BBC reports. The Shard is western Europe's tallest building, at 1,016 feet, and it's in sight of the three London bases of Shell, which is involved in Arctic drilling. The climbers, all women, were live-streaming the event from the top of the skyscraper using their helmets. "It is an honor to stand here at the foot of Europe's highest building and witness this remarkable achievement by these women," Greenpeace UK's executive director said in a statement. "And I'm not the only one watching this today—the executives ofShell, whose offices are all around this building, simply won't be able to ignore what we have done." The Shard was completed last year, and a spokesman for the building says it's "being used by protesters as part of a campaign," the Guardian reports. "We are working with the relevant authorities to try to ensure the safety of those concerned."

– Police in Mountain View, Calif., have arrested a 41-year-old man accused of knocking over a 300-pound security robot in a parking lot, ABC7 reports. According to CNET, Jason Sylvain was arrested for prowling and public intoxication after allegedly knocking over the Knightscope K5 security robot last Thursday. The 5-foot-tall robot can read 300 license plates per minute and provides 360-degree video streaming. It's also not armed. "The robot did exactly as it was supposed to do—the 'assault' was detected and immediately reported. The alarms on the robot sounded, the suspect attempted to flee the scene and was detained by one of my colleagues and me until the Mountain View Police arrived," Knightscope VP of marketing and sales Stacy Dean Stephens tells CNET. Stephens says the robot suffered only scratches and is now back on patrol. Police say Sylvain "appeared confused, had red, glassy eyes, and a strong odor of alcohol emitted from him," according to a police spokesperson. Knightscope says it's a testament to the technology that police caught the aggressor and arrested him. One local resident tells ABC7 he would've arrested Sylvain or given him a ticket for attacking the robot. Another resident says the incident shows "how spineless the drunk guys in Silicon Valley really are because they attack a victim who doesn't even have any arms." Knightscope is the company behind the robot that ran over a toddler's foot at a Stanford shopping center last year.

– The San Andreas Fault is overdue for a major earthquake, according to a new study. Researchers studying a 100-mile stretch of the fault near Los Angeles say it's been 160 years since the last major quake along the fault, which runs from the Antelope Valley to the Tejon Pass, reports the Los Angeles Times. The study found that there have been 10 major quakes in the area over the past 1,200 years, most of them magnitude 7.5 or greater. "The stress is building up along the San Andreas and a large earthquake is inevitable," USGS geophysicist Robert Graves tells KABC-TV. "Large, but less damaging earthquakes" are more typical along that stretch of fault, he says. Researchers say the study should not be seen as a reason to panic, but as a warning to people to be prepared. "This is not a time to panic," Graves tells the Times. "It's not time to freak out." He says that if there was a major quake, it would be enough to shut off power to Los Angeles, cut supplies to the area, and "make it difficult to get power to LA, get supplies in and out of Interstate 5, for example." The study, published in the Geophysical Research Letters journal, focused on an area of the Fault that hadn't been studied in detail before, the New York Times reports. The researchers found that the area had been hit by several earthquakes between the late 1800s and early 1900s, but none of them were as powerful as those that hit the area in recent years.

– Kellogg has become the latest American company to leave Venezuela, and President Nicolas Maduro says his government has seized the company's factory in Maracay, the BBC reports. "I've decided to hand the company over to the workers so that they can continue producing for the people," Maduro said at a campaign rally Monday. "We've begun judicial proceedings against the business leaders of Kellogg's because their exit is unconstitutional," he added, per Reuters. Kellogg announced Tuesday it was closing the factory, which employed 400 workers, citing the "deterioration" of the country's economy, the Wall Street Journal reports. The company said it had written down the value of its Venezuela holdings and would like to return to the country in the future. "If the Venezuelan government takes control of Kimberly-Clark facilities and operations, it will be responsible for the well-being of the workers and the physical asset, equipment, and machinery in the facilities going forward," the company said in a statement.

– Sudanese President Omar al-Bashir left South Africa on Monday despite a judge's order that he be arrested on charges of war crimes, crimes against humanity, and genocide, Reuters reports. The judge ordered all ports in the country to keep al-bashir from leaving, but lawyers arguing for his arrest warned in advance that would not be the case, CNN reports. Sudan's State Minister for Information told Reuters that Bashir had flown home and was expected to land in the Sudanese capital of Khartoum at around 6:30pm local time. The International Criminal Court issued an arrest warrant for Bashir in 2009 and another in 2010, NBC News reports. He has evaded arrest for years and was facing the toughest challenge to his freedom yet in South Africa, where he was attending an African Union summit when a judge barred him from leaving the country pending a hearing on whether to hand him over to the ICC. The decision to let Bashir leave represented an affirmation of shifting diplomatic priorities for the government, with African interests trumping those of the West. It also represented a blow for the Hague-based ICC, which has convicted just two minor African warlords since it started work in 2002 and has struggled to create accountability for those who are too powerful to be tried at home.

– For the second year in a row, Coca Cola ran a Super Bowl ad on TV and online at the same time. This year, the company's "Coke Chase" campaign involved three teams—Cowboys, Vegas Show Girls, and Mad Max types—competing in a race to get a giant bottle of Coke. Viewers could vote for their favorite team or "sabotage" them by throwing obstacles in their way, Mashable reports. Since the company unveiled the campaign on Jan. 22, 1.3 million people visited the CokeChase.com website, according to a Coke rep. Coke received 910,000 votes for the campaign and 7,3 million sabotages. While the engagement numbers per visitor seem strong, it appears fewer people took part in the online campaign than the previous year. For the 2012 Super Bowl, Coke ran a live feed during the game showing the company’s famous polar bears watching and reacting to the game. Coke later revealed that some 9 million people had checked-in across various platforms to see the polar bears. Part of the problem, as Mashable reported Sunday night, was that the voting and video features stopped working for many users while the game was going on. But it appears the 60-second spot for Coke Chase was significantly more popular than the videos for polar bear campaign in 2012. Coke says this year's 60- seconds spot has been viewed more than 2.5 million times, while the Polar Bowl spots were viewed 1.7 million times collectively.

– San Francisco's 58-story Millennium Tower has sunk 17 inches since construction began in 2005, and it's also tilting 14 inches to the northwest, the San Francisco Chronicle reports. According to CBS News, the tower opened in 2009 as the tallest residential building west of the Mississippi. Since then, some residents say they've lost up to $3 million selling their condos. The tower is currently the subject of numerous lawsuits from residents, who blame the adjacent Transbay Transit Center for failing to "protect existing structures from excessive ground movements during construction." But the tower had already sunk 10 inches by the time construction on the transit center began in 2010. Now, a new report shows the tower has sunk yet another inch in the past seven months. A pair of engineering firms hired by developer Millennium Partners and other parties involved in the dispute think they have a solution that will stabilize the tower and prop it back upright. The firms estimate the fix will cost $100 million to $150 million—more than your average home foundation repair, but a lot less than the billion-dollar-plus price tag that some experts have feared. Some residents have already sold their units. "We don't know if this building's going to stand up in an earthquake," says Frank Jernigan. "And so I became severely frightened of that." Jerry Dodson, an engineer and lawyer, says he makes daily rounds of the basement looking for signs of deterioration. "There's enough of them, a spider web of cracks, that you have to be concerned about what's going on underneath," he tells 60 Minutes.

– The founder of Pakistan's nuclear weapons program claims that in the late 1990s North Korean officials paid kickbacks to senior Pakistani military figures in exchange for critical weapons technology. Abdul Qadeer Khan has given a US-based expert documents that appear to show North Korea's government paid more than $3.5 million to two Pakistani military officials as part of the deal, the expert tells the AP. To back up his claim, Khan released what he said was a copy of a North Korean official's 1998 letter to him, written in English, that purports to describe the secret deal. Khan gave the documents to Simon Henderson of the Washington Institute for Near East Policy, an authority on Pakistan's weapons program. He did so because he has been accused by his government of running a covert nuclear smuggling operation without official knowledge or consent. Henderson says the documents prove Khan's claims that his nuclear arms smuggling network had high-level support from the Pakistani government, but others say the letter bolsters the government's claims it didn't know what Khan was up to. Pakistani officials, including those named as recipients of the cash, have called the letter a fake. Khan has been freed from home detention but remains under round-the-clock surveillance in a suburb of Islamabad, where the government has recently threatened him with new sanctions for illicit communications. If that's true, it could deepen the distrust between the United States and Pakistan, which are struggling to set aside their differences and cooperate in the battle against militant extremists in Afghanistan and Pakistan.

– You get what you pay for when it comes to health care in the US, according to a new report from the Commonwealth Fund. The think tank ranks the US dead last out of 11 industrialized nations for the quality of health care based on efficiency, equity, access to care, and outcomes, reports the Washington Post. The UK, which has a single-payer health care system, is first overall, while the US came in last or near-last in all three categories. The report looked at the health systems of Australia, Canada, France, Germany, the Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Sweden, Switzerland, and the UK. The US spent more per capita on health care than any other country but came in dead last when it came to outcomes, the Atlantic reports. The data for the report was collected before the full implementation of ObamaCare, which dramatically expanded health insurance, so it's possible that the US may rise in future rankings.

– A 1-year-old girl died Wednesday after being left in a car seat all day in a pickup truck parked at her family's home in East Nashville, reports the Washington Post. Police say the incident marks at least the seventh instance in which a child has died in a hot car this year, according to data from NoHeatStroke.org, which has been tracking child vehicular heat-stroke deaths since 1998. Matt Barker left home at 7:30am Wednesday with his two children, intending to take them to day care a few miles away, police say. He returned to his home in the East Nashville area about a half hour later and left a few minutes after that via a ride-share service for Nashville International Airport, where he flew out on a business trip. His wife, Jenny, left for her job in another vehicle thinking that both children were at the day care center. She went to the center in the evening and was told that Katera never had been dropped off. She called her husband and realized the child was still in the pickup truck. She found KaterA in the truck and began CPR until paramedics arrived. She was pronounced dead at Vanderbilt University Medical Center. The high yesterday was 89 degrees, which means the interior of the truck could've easily reached between 130-170 degrees, per WTVM. Police are not filing charges at this time.

– Passengers aboard a United Airlines flight from Hong Kong to Newark Liberty International Airport say they restrained a "paranoid" passenger who screamed about being poisoned by the FBI and wanted the flight to be diverted to Canada. Passengers say the man, who was restrained by other passengers and a flight attendant, started screaming about nine hours into the 15-hour flight, the AP reports. "He was clearly not stable," says passenger Jacques Roizen, who helped wrestle the man to the floor and sat in the same row as him after he was restrained. The FBI was waiting for the flight when it landed, and the man was taken into custody. The man's name has not been released, but passengers describe him as American. Passenger Peter Jones says the man called out what he said was his name, his birthdate, and his Social Security number several times and said he worked for the US Embassy in Abu Dhabi. Jones said the man claimed to have information about Edward Snowden, a former US government contractor who leaked a once-secret National Security Agency surveillance program two weeks ago. Roizen says he and another passenger sat by the man for the remaining six hours trying to calm him by talking about their families, Father's Day, and their children. After a passenger told him his actions had scared the children, "he started crying because he said, 'I don't want to hurt the children.' He asked both of us to put him under arrest, asked us to read him his rights."

– With polls showing Donald Trump's lead in the Rust Belt shrinking, the New York Times reports that Republicans may be forced to decide in the next few days whether to distance themselves from their nominee. The Times spoke to a dozen strategists from both parties, who say Trump's support has plunged across the swing-state map over the last 10 days, wiping out his recovery from September and threatening to undo weeks of Republican gains in the battle for control of Congress. Trump has already slipped perceptibly in public polls, trailing widely this week in Pennsylvania and by smaller margins in Florida and North Carolina—three states he cannot afford to lose. Politico, meanwhile, reports that Trump's hopes for victory are not much different from Mitt Romney's four years ago, or what Jeb Bush's or Marco Rubio's might have been: a narrow path that relies on success out West, a new national bellwether marked by a growing Hispanic population. “The path to 270 goes through all the same states as it did in 2012 and 2008,” says Katie Packer, who was Romney’s deputy campaign manager in 2008. "Michigan, Wisconsin, and Pennsylvania aren't really even swing states. The math still comes down to suburban women and Latino voters.”

– Forget passwords. A new study out of Binghamton University in New York suggests that your brainwaves could be used to verify your identity, reports Tech Times. In the study, published in the journal Neurocomputing, researchers observed and recorded the brain signals of 45 volunteers as they read a list of 75 acronyms, such as FBI and DVD. The reaction of the region of the brain associated with reading and recognizing words was different for each acronym and unique to each volunteer. The difference was so significant that a computer was able to identify each volunteer with 94% accuracy. The results were also stable over time, with identification possible after a lag of up to six months. Sarah Laszlo, co-author of "Brainprint," says that brain biometrics are appealing because they are cancellable and cannot be stolen by malicious means the way a finger or retina can. "If someone's fingerprint is stolen, that person can't just grow a new finger to replace the compromised fingerprint—the fingerprint for that person is compromised forever," she says. "Fingerprints are 'non-cancellable.' Brainprints, on the other hand, are potentially cancellable. So, in the unlikely event that attackers were actually able to steal a brainprint from an authorized user, the authorized user could then 'reset' their brainprint." The researchers don't see brainprints as a potential replacement for passwords for low security applications in the near future—after all, who wants to hook themselves up to an electroencephalograph (EEG) just to log into their email—but they do see the technology having potential in high security environments. "We tend to see the applications of this system as being more along the lines of high-security physical locations, like the Pentagon or Air Force Labs, where there aren't that many users that are authorized to enter, and those users don't need to constantly be authorizing the way that a consumer might need to authorize into their phone or computer," Zhanpeng Jin says in a press release.

– The police officer who shot Michael Brown in Ferguson, Mo., didn't stop him because he was suspected of robbing a convenience store, but because Brown was "walking down the middle of the street blocking traffic," the city's police chief said today, per CNN. Ferguson Police Chief Thomas Jackson also said the robbery "does not relate to the initial contact between the officer and Michael Brown." Jackson said he released surveillance video of the incident "because the press asked for it," adding that he couldn't keep it locked up indefinitely. The chief added that "we needed to release that at the same time we need to release the name of the officer involved in the shooting," though he didn't elaborate on why. Meanwhile, Dylann Roof's uncle tells the Wall Street Journal that his nephew had an interest in white supremacy and that his father bought him a pistol for his birthday this year.

– When the Nazis razed the Treblinka death camp in 1943, all that remained was a forest and a fake train station, LiveScience reports. So forensic archaeologist Caroline Sturdy Colls and her team set out to explore the site using a new technology: lidar. Using lasers to measure the distance between the ground and an airplane-borne instrument, the team was able to see what they thought might be the camp's original foundation. What they found: two previously unknown mass graves measuring 63 feet by 58 feet, as well as shoes, ammunition, and bones with cut marks. "Treblinka had never been looked at since the period after the war," Colls tells LiveScience. "And everybody had assumed that because the history books said it was destroyed, it was." What they also found: a gas chamber, evidence that the Nazis had tried to cover up their crimes. "For me, that was quite shocking," says Colls. "These artifacts are there, and these human remains are on the surface, and they're not being recorded or recovered." What's more, the gas chambers were the only brick buildings in the camp, and the orange tiles on the floor were stamped with the Star of David, suggesting they were used to hide the fact that the camp was a Jewish bathhouse, the New York Times reports. What the Times has to say about the show, which airs Saturday on the Smithsonian Channel: It's "heartwarming to see the reverence with which they treated the remains, which were subsequently reburied under the rabbi’s supervision," writes Curt Schleier. But the show "could stand to lose a few little lapses," he adds, noting that the producers used reality show music and used devices "designed to maximize the sense of impending drama. It was fine with Al Capone's vault. Here it's just insulting."

– The CIA has concluded that Russia's goal in interfering in the election wasn't to undermine confidence in the electoral system but to help Donald Trump win, officials briefed on the matter tell the Washington Post. "It is the assessment of the intelligence community that Russia’s goal here was to favor one candidate over the other, to help Trump get elected," a senior official says. "That's the consensus view." Intelligence agencies have identified individuals with connections to the Russian government who provided WikiLeaks with thousands of hacked emails from the Democratic National Committee and others, including Hillary Clinton's campaign chairman, according to US officials. Many of the documents were also provided to WikiLeaks, which released them over many weeks before the Nov. 8 election. The finding about the Republican committee is expected to be included in a detailed report of "lessons learned" that President Obama has ordered intelligence agencies to assemble before he leaves office on Jan. 20. The Trump transition team dismissed the findings in a short statement issued Friday evening. "These are the same people that said Saddam Hussein had weapons of mass destruction," the statement read. "The election ended a long time ago in one of the biggest Electoral College victories in history. It's now time to move on and 'Make America Great Again.'" Sen. Chuck Schumer says Senate Democrats will join with Republican colleagues next year to demand a congressional investigation and hearings to get to the bottom of this, the Post reports.

– A preschool in Hawaii is taking a closer look at its snack policy after three children took sips of Pine-Sol, the AP reports. Officials say a classroom assistant at a Honolulu preschool mistook the household cleaner for apple juice. Paramedics were called to the Kilohana United Methodist Church Preschool to evaluate three girls, ages 4 and 5, on Tuesday morning. Par paramedics found no signs of trauma or sickness, and the girls' parents declined to take them to the hospital. The preschool says only sips were taken before the lead teacher realized the liquid was not apple juice, KHON reports. Inspectors say the assistant picked up the container of yellow-brown liquid off a cleanup cart in the kitchen and poured it into cups. The cleaner was its original container and properly labeled.

– The mysterious deaths of Toronto billionaires Barry and Honey Sherman have been classified as a double homicide, police announced Friday. "I believe that they were targeted," Det.-Sgt. Susan Gomes said at a news conference, per the CBC. The couple, who were found dead in their home on Dec. 15, were found with belts around their necks attached to a railing near their indoor pool, the Wall Street Journal reports. There were no signs of forced entry into the home, and police say they've spent thousands of hours investigating the case, seizing more than 150 pieces of evidence, 127 witness interviews, and reviewing 2,000 hours of surveillance footage and video from nearby homes and businesses. Police say they have an "extensive list" of people to interview, but Gomes wouldn't talk about possible suspects. She did, however, confirm that they have a list of "persons of interest" who had access to the home through the realtor's "lock box," the Toronto Star reports. The home was removed from the market five days after the couple's bodies were found. The family of Barry Sherman, founder of generic drugmaker Apotex, has hired an independent investigation.

– Donald Trump's decision to skip the final debate before the Iowa caucuses "is rooted in one thing—Megyn Kelly, whom he has viciously attacked since August and has now spent four days demanding be removed from the debate stage," Fox News says in a statement. "We can't give in to terrorizations toward any of our employees. Trump is still welcome at Thursday night's debate and will be treated fairly, just as he has been during his 132 appearances on Fox News & FOX Business, but he can't dictate the moderators or the questions." The network says it's "surprised he's willing to show that much fear about being questioned by Megyn Kelly," per the New York Times, which notes that Trump's campaign manager, Corey Lewandowski, said on MSNBC that he doesn't "think" Trump will change his mind about attending Thursday's debate. "What you have with Mr. Trump, that you have a clear leader. A person who he says at best is not going to be toyed with," Lewandowski said, per the Times. "I just don't see in any way where Fox is going to have a successful debate now, when you got the clear G.O.P. front-runner who has decided to walk away from this debate because he can’t be treatedfairly at the debatestage." Fox says it has "warned" Lewandowski "not to level any more threats" against Kelly, per Business Insider, which points out that the network has previously warned Trump that "the Ayatollah and Putin both intend to treat Donald Trump unfairly when they meet with him if he becomes president." In a statement, Fox News calls Trump's move a "pathetic attempt" by the network to build up ratings, per Mediaite. "Like running for office as an extremely successful person, this takes guts and it is the kind mentality our country needs in order to Make America Great Again," Trump says in his own statement, per Politico. "This is a stunt you'd pull if you were desperate—not when you're dominant." The debate will go on with or without Trump, the network says. The AP notes that the other seven GOP candidates will be in an "awkward position" if they attack Trump and give him even more attention, or ignore him and leave voters wondering what they think of the hardball play just days before the Feb. 1 caucus.

– Rescue crews using bulldozers and their bare hands raced to dig out survivors from a strong earthquake that reduced three central Italian towns to rubble early Wednesday. The death toll stood at 120 but the number of dead and missing was uncertain given the huge number of vacationers in the area for summer's final days. Residents wakened before dawn by the temblor emerged from their crumbled homes to find what they described as apocalyptic scenes "like Dante's Inferno," with entire blocks of buildings turned into piles of sand and rock, thick dust choking the air, and a putrid smell of gas, the AP reports. "The town isn't here anymore," said Sergio Pirozzi, the mayor of the hardest-hit town, Amatrice. "I believe the toll will rise." The magnitude 6.2 quake struck at 3:36am and was felt across a broad swath of central Italy, including Rome, where residents woke to a long swaying followed by aftershocks. The tembler shook the Lazio region and Umbria and Le Marche on the Adriatic coast. Premier Matteo Renzi visited the zone Wednesday, greeted rescue teams and survivors, and announced the toll stands at 120. He promised the quake-prone area that "No family, no city, no hamlet will be left behind." Italy's civil protection agency, which was coordinating the rescue and care for survivors, dozens were injured and thousands in need of temporary housing, though it stressed the numbers were fluid. Dozens were pulled out alive: There was relief as a woman emerged on a stretcher from one building, followed by a dog. And wails when a corpse was extracted. A steady column of dump trucks brought tons of twisted metal, rock, and cement down the hill and onto the highway toward Rome, along with a handful of ambulances bringing the injured to Rome hospitals. "We need chain saws, shears to cut iron bars and jacks to remove beams. Everything, we need everything," civil protection worker Andrea Gentili told the AP in the early hours of the recovery. The New York Times has a primer on why Italy is so prone to earthquakes.

– Republicans have a good shot at taking control of the Senate in November, but they'll have to do it "state by state by state," writes Nate Silver in the Washington Post. Republicans need to gain six seats, and three states—Montana, South Dakota, and West Virginia—are almost certain to switch from Democrat to Republican. The debate, however, is about how many more Republicans will win beyond that. Republicans have plenty of options, but "their victories must come either by defeating Democratic incumbents in red states or picking off seats in blue or purple states that Barack Obama and the Democrats have been winning in presidential elections," writes Silver. Democrats, on the other hand, see their candidates as doing well and GOP challengers as weaker than expected. They also have the "invisible ground game," in the form of more money spent identifying, registering, and turning out voters. Republicans are in an "enviable but not yet commanding position," and "over the next months they will have to earn it." Click for Silver's full column.

– Seven years after Madeleine McCann disappeared while on vacation with her parents in Portugal, Scotland Yard detectives are preparing to dig up three sites near the Praia da Luz resort using radar equipment, reports the Daily Mirror. "There will be earth diggers everywhere and it will look very dramatic," says a source close to the parents. "It will be a heartbreaking and hugely emotional time for madeleine’s poor parents." The parents, Kate and Gerry McCann, have been told of the plans but are not expected to travel to Portugal, the Guardian reports. A Metropolitan Police spokesman would neither confirm nor deny the report. "We are not going to give a running commentary," he said. "From the beginning of Operation Grange, we have made it clear that speculation has been unhelpful to the investigation and that continues to be the case. As we have said from the beginning, we will not be commenting on any speculation surrounding this investigation." Police said last month that they knew of nine sexual assaults and three "near misses" on British girls between the ages of 6 and 12 who were on holiday in the Algarve between 2004 and 2006. Five new cases in which young girls were assaulted and one near miss came to light as a result of an appeal in March for information about a lone predator entering rooms of British families on holiday. The Met said more than 500 people came forward and its officers are cautiously optimistic that they will be soon be working on the ground with Portuguese authorities, who will remain in charge of the investigation despite hopes of a joint operation.

– Seth Rogen's mom, Sandy, tweeted Wednesday, "Falling asleep after sex is like shavasana after yoga.!," Mashable reports. According to the Wall Street Journal, more than 40,000 people retweeted the tweet, and by Thursday morning, Rogen had responded to it: "Jesus f---ing Christ mom." Sandy's response: "It's just an observation of life !" Seth's reply: "Whateverrrrrrrr mom mm!" Sandy's reply to that: "it's gnarly." The rest of the Rogen family chimed in, Mashable notes: "Lame!" "Moments like this are why Twitter was invented."

– Edward Conard is a member of the 0.1%—that is, a man whose wealth is probably in the hundreds of millions and who is one of the largest donors to Mitt Romney—and he's spent the last four years writing a book that he hopes "will forever change the way we view the superrich’s role in our society," writes Adam Davidson in the New York Times. In Unintended Consequences, Conard argues that income inequality is a good thing, a sign that the US economy is working, and that we should appreciate the wealth of others more, because we're all benefiting proportionally from it. "This could be the most hated book of the year," Davidson writes. Conard says that while many economists believe society benefits from investments by the wealthy, he argues that for every dollar an investor earns, the public receives the equivalent of $20 in value. He cites the example of computers, which he says have helped everyone get a lot more value by competing to improve their products and lower prices. He also argues that investment banks, with their complicated financial derivatives and overleveraged balance sheets, make the economy more efficient, and he advocates creating a new government program that guarantees to bail out the banks if they ever face another run. Click for Davidson's full column.

– Colorado is home to 3.7 million square feet of industrial space, and one in every 11 of those spaces is now occupied by marijuana growers, according to a new report from commercial real estate firm CBRE. The Denver Post reports that between 2009 and 2014, marijuana cultivation accounted for more than a third of all industrial space leased in the city. "It really kick-started the recovery of the industrial market in Denver," says a CBRE analyst. But the marijuana industry isn't the only reason housing prices are rising in Denver. Real estate broker Jason Thomas tells the Post that marijuana growers are paying up to three times the rent of other businesses in order to lease space in buildings. He says the industry is "making a huge impact, some positive, some negative, to mainstream businesses." But an urban planning professor at the University of Colorado Denver disagrees. She tells the Denver Post that high costs of living in coastal cities like Boston, New York, San Francisco, and LA have prompted higher-paid workers to move to second-tier cities like Denver. And millennials are also moving to the city for its "recreational opportunities." Thomas says marijuana leasing could have some interesting consequences on Denver's development cycle, since many buildings are locked into leases for the next five to 20 years and "off the potential redevelopment list." The Atlantic reports that Denver is "t treading water: trying to regulate growth it didn’t plan for, and reaping the benefits of cannabis sales without studying their long-term effects. If history is any indication, we’ll go on madly lurching toward expansive profits in this gold rush, the cost to Denver’s long- term residents and culture be damned."

– Tina Fey and Amy Poehler reunite for Sisters, about two sisters forced to clean out their childhood bedroom before their parents sell the house. Critics aren't exactly enamored. Here's what they're saying: "Sisters is as loosey-goosey as a movie can get without falling apart, but the amusing twosome's chemistry mostly overcomes the chaos," writes AO Scott at the New York Times. "Fey and Poehler have never relied more on vulgarity and stretched-out slapstick shtick for easy laughs than they do here. Yet they have no trouble taking the audience along for an LOL ride, because they make you feel like you’re eavesdropping on old friends drinking too much wine at the next table. No matter what nonsense they spout, their mood is infectious." It's "really bad fun that’s badly directed (by Jason Moore), badly written (by Paula Pell), badly performed, and bad news for anyone who cares about the quality of big-screen comedy," writes Joe Morgenstern at the Wall Street Journal. The movie is "so much fun that it's hard to believe it's a movie," he writes. "It's like watching your friends throw a party at your house, only this time it's at your sister's house." The plot is "riddled with more holes than an age-inappropriate party dress," writes Bruce Kirkland at the Toronto Sun. "While all this nonsense is going on, personal issues are being dealt with—but strictly in a superficial way—while Fey and Poehl and company get trashed, crude, profane, childish, and idiotic. If you think that sounds like a good thing, meet the Sisters. Otherwise, find something else to do." The film "never gets its act together while mashing up fragments of the comedy classic Animal House and playing it out like a drug-and-alcohol-fuelled episode of Desperate Housewives," writes Manohla Dargis at the Washington Post. "By turns gently ribald and unapologetically filthy, this exercise in escalating fits of mortification often resembles a spin on Trainwreck, which had anxious fans fearing the hilarious pair had lost their touch."

– The city of Deerfield Beach, Fla., is debating whether to build townhouses on a 3-acre plot of land that used to be a black cemetery, the South Florida Sun-Sentinel reports. Some 40 years ago, angry residents stopped plans to build apartments on the site, and two studies have concluded that there are no bodies at the site. "I saw it with my own eyes, I am a living eyewitness," says 83-year-old Benjamin Miller, who had family and friends buried there. If bodies were moved, "they didn't notify me, and they didn’t notify my mother and they never notified my daddy. They were living, they were next of kin. Where did they put the bodies?" Mayor Jean Robb says she thinks they were moved to the city-owned Pineview Cemetery, but she hasn't been able to confirm that hunch. "It's been undeveloped for years. It will add to the tax rolls," says Robb. "Why leave it empty?" The first phases of the development were approved last night, but commissioners backed off after residents testified that they had seen burials there. "This project is great, but this is very disturbing," says one commissioner. Another adds, "I'm very uncomfortable voting."

– A 21-year-old Navy SEAL trainee died during his first week of basic training in Coronado, Calif., on Friday, the AP reports. Seaman James Derek Lovelace was doing what's known as "drown-proofing," which NBC News calls "one of the world's most challenging military training regimens," when instructors noticed he was having trouble treading in a camouflage uniform and dive mask. He lost consciousness and was pulled out of the pool; emergency responders were unable to revive him and he was pronounced dead at a nearby hospital. The Virginian-Pilot first reported the death, but the Navy didn't officially announce it until it was contacted by NBC and the Pilot on Tuesday. A Navy spokesperson says the death is now under investigation. The Washington Post reports that the SEAL training exercise in question was an "legendarily difficult" one that tests a student's ability to survive in water. In a statement, the Navy says that "safety observers identified Lovelaces as having difficulty and withdrew him from the exercise. He was aided to the edge of the swimming pool by instructors where he then lost consciousness. Resuscitation efforts and first aid at the scene were unsuccessful." A Navy official tells the Pilot that the exercise is "a physiological test to see if a guy can do exercises in the water." The Navy bio for LovelACE, who was born in Germany, says he studied mechanical engineering at Faulkner State Community College in Alabama before enlisting in the Navy in November. His mother died in 2015 at the age of 44, the San Diego Union-Tribune reports. He leaves behind a father and two sisters.

– An opera singer is suing an Army hospital after she says a nurse-midwife botched her childbirth operation, the Army Times reports. Amy Herbst says the nurse at Blanchfield Army Community Hospital at Fort Campbell, Ky., performed an episiotomy on her in the second stage of labor without her knowledge. The wound was repaired with sutures, but after Herbst left the hospital, she says she began to experience "fecal urgency and incontinence, including periodic leaking of stool and excessive flatulence." A second nurse told her that attempts to repair the cut had been unsuccessful, and a colorectal surgeon told her she would need reconstructive surgery to fix the damage. Herbst, a professional opera singer, says the surgery has left her unable to continue her career. “She is suffering though a very embarrassing and very significant injury, and frankly, the prognosis of a fully successful repair is pretty low,” says her attorney. A hospital spokesperson declined to comment on the specifics of the case, but said the hospital is deeply concerned with its patients' outcomes.

– For the first time, President Obama has publicly acknowledged that US forces have taken "direct action" against al-Qaeda-affiliated groups in Somalia and Yemen, reports the New York Times. In a letter to Congress, Obama said American forces had engaged in "a limited number" of operations against members of the Shabab in Somali and al-Qaida in the Arabian Peninsula in Yemen, arguing that both posed a terrorist threat to “the United States and our interests.” He gave no further details in the unclassified letter, which accompanied the latest update to lawmakers under the War Powers Act about military operations around the world. More details about the scope of the operations were included in the classified section of the report, administration officials say. The report applies only to US military operations, including those by special operations forces, not those conducted by the CIA, the AP notes. The decision to declassify the existence of the counterterror actions in those two countries amounts "an incremental move toward greater openness about the use of US force overseas," says the AP. "It does not reflect any change in the intensity or basic character of the US campaign to defeat al- Qaeda."

– Mitt Romney's Facebook page is losing 593 friends per hour, or about 11 likes per minute, according to the Daily Dot. The Washington Post first noticed it on Friday, and Mashable says it's now up to 847 friends an hour. The site DisappearingRomney.com, which tracks Romney's loss of Facebook friends in real time, notes that Romney still has more than 12 million Facebook fans and 1.7 million followers on Twitter, but he hasn't posted anything on either site since 6pm on Election Day. President Obama, on the other hand, has posted two celebratory tweets since the election, CNN notes.

– "I heard footsteps above me, and I checked in with my landlord, because sometimes he's used the storage above," an Arlington, Va., woman tells NBC Washington. Her landlord alerted police around 1:30am Friday, and officers soon found a 60-year-old homeless man living in the attic. Anthony Jones was charged with unlawful entry and is being held without bail. Police say Jones is believed to have entered the house through a sliding glass door. "It would be scary for anyone," a police spokeswoman says, but the tenant says police acted quickly and made her feel "much safer." The Arlington Daily News has a list of the most recent crimes in the area.

– Catherine Zeta-Jones and Michael Douglas are splitting up, sources tell People. The couple hasn't been photographed together since April, and "the stress has taken a toll on their marriage," a friend says. A spokesperson for the couple, married since 2000, says they "are taking some time apart to evaluate and work on theirmarriage," the AP reports. They have two children.

– Chen Guangcheng says he's confident China will honor its end of a deal allowing the blind dissident to study in the US. "Since the Chinese government has promised to safeguard my constitutionally provided rights and freedom and safety, I feel that they will fulfill their commitments because it is after all an agreement between two countries," Chen tells the AP. He says he has asked hospital staff to help him obtain travel documents, but American officials haven't been able to visit him in the hospital and he has been barred from meeting friends and US diplomats. Chen says his family is still under threat, and he wants officials in his home village in Shandong province to investigate his accusations of false imprisonment and abuse, Reuters reports. "My lawyer has a lot of evidence from back then, so I hope he'll be able to take part in the central government's investigation," he says. "You can't let them escape from legal punishment because it was led by a party secretary and done by officials."

– When Barbara Bezant and Lyn Jeffries were 17 and 19, respectively, they saw a Beatles concert in London and recorded a message for Paul McCartney. "This dream is just to come round the back and see you, but I don't suppose that'll ever happen," they wrote on a reel-to-reel tape. "But we can always live in hope, can't we?" They sent the tape to the Finsbury Park Astoria, where the Beatles were to play the next night, the BBC reports. Decades later, a local historian bought the tape at a flea market and listened to it. "It was like sitting in a bubble and being there when these two girls were making this tape," he says. "The hairs on the back of my neck stood up and it made me wonder if these two were still with us." The One Show reunited the women at the Beatles Story exhibition in Liverpool and presented them with Sir Paul's reply. "Hi Linda and Barbara, thanks very much for you lovely tape. It finally got through, better late than never," he wrote. "Great to hear that you found each other after all these years. Keep enjoying the music, love Paul." The women, who hadn't seen each other in 40 years, said they were "amazed" to be reunited and to finally get a reply from a Beatle.

– A married Christian couple in Pakistan were killed and their bodies burned in the brick kiln where they worked after being accused of desecrating the Koran, AFP reports. The couple, identified only by their first names, Shama and Shehzad, were working in the town of Kot Radha Kishan southwest of Lahore when they were attacked by a mob. "A mob of several dozen attacked the building where they were," Mushtaq Gill, chief advocate at Pakistani minority rights group LEAD, tells NBC News. "They broke their legs so they couldn't run and then threw them in the fire. Only some bones and hair were found at the site." Gill says the couple had planned to flee their town with their three young children. Police say up to 35 people were involved in the attack and arrests are being made. Blasphemy is a very sensitive issue in Pakistan, where even unproven allegations can lead to mob violence. A Christian woman on death row since 2010 was found guilty of making derogatory remarks about the Prophet Muhammad after an argument with a Muslim woman, and an elderly British man sentenced to death for blasphemy was shot by a prison guard last month.

– The Trump administration is considering staying in the Paris agreement to fight climate change "under the right conditions," offering to re-engage in the international deal three months after President Trump said the US would pull out if it didn't find more favorable terms, reports the Wall Street Journal. During a climate-change meeting Saturday of more than 30 ministers led by Canada, China, and the European Union in Montreal, US officials broached revising US climate- change goals, two participants said, signaling a compromise. The Journal's story is based on accounts in the Washington Post, the New York Times, and Bloomberg. The Post's sources say the US is looking to revise its goals to make them more palatable to Trump, who has promised to renegotiate or scrap the Paris deal. Trump tweeted Sunday morning: "The United States will be a major partner in the fight against climate change!"

– "Great, now I can go after Horseface and her 3rd rate lawyer in the Great State of Texas. She will confirm the letter she signed! She knows nothing about me, a total con!" That was President Trump's Tuesday morning tweet referring to Stormy Daniels, whose defamation lawsuit against him was thrown out Monday, Politico reports. Daniels' lawyer, Michael Avenatti, was quick to respond to the president, calling him a "disgusting misogynist and an embarrassment to the United States." "Bring everything you have, because we are going to demonstrate to the world what a complete shyster and liar you are," Avenatti tweeted. "How many other women did you cheat on your wife with while you had a baby at home?" Daniels, who claims she had an affair with Trump in 2006, also used her own nickname for him, NBC News reports. "Game on, Tiny," she tweeted. In an interview with the AP later Monday, Trump did not back down from calling Daniels "horseface." "You can take it any way you want," he said when asked if it was appropriate to insult a woman's appearance. In her book Full Disclosure, released this month, Daniels described sex with Trump as "the least impressive sex I’d ever had" and detailed his sex organs in graphic detail. She later said she regretted "body shaming" him.

– Today is the 52nd anniversary of John F. Kennedy's assassination, and the Upper East Side auction house Doyle New York is selling letters he wrote to Swedish socialite Gunilla von Post, whom he fell for weeks before he married Jackie. The letters, which date from 1954 to 1956, provide a glimpse into the steamy affair between the then-senator and the socialite he met in Cannes, France, in 1953, the New York Post reports. They include two complete, signed letters on Senate stationery, as well as two partial letters, and three hand-written envelopes. A previous cache of letters between the pair sold at auction for $115,000 in 2010, and a set was found among von Post's belongings after her death in 2011 at age 79. Together, the two bundles make up all of the lovers' known letters. The earliest letter in the current lot, dated 1954, reveals JFK's attempt to meet up in Stockholm. "Is there any chance you shall be there as I would like to say hello," he writes. "I am anxious to see you—is it not strange after all these months? Perhaps at first it shall be a little difficult as we shall be strangers—but not strangers . . . It is a long way to Gunilla—it is worth it." Their affair, which continued after his wedding to Jackie, reached a head in August 1955, when the two spent a week together shacking up around Sweden, von Post says in her 1997 memoir, Love, Jack. The memoir details JFK's attempts to end his marriage and bring von Post to America—efforts that would be thwarted by his father, political ambition, and sensitivity to Jackie's miscarriage in 1955 and pregnancy in 1956.

– Mitt Romney and Donald Trump went at it again on Thursday, with Romney accusing Trump of "bullying, the greed, the showing off, the misogyny, the absurd third-grade theatrics," CNN reports. "Here's what I know: Donald Trump is a phony, a fraud," Romney said. "His promises are as worthless as a degree from Trump University. He's playing members of the American public for suckers: He gets a free ride to the White House, and all we get is a lousy hat." He said Trump's plan to let Russia lead the fight against ISIS is "the most ridiculous and dangerous idea of the entire campaign season." Romney said Trump was begging for his endorsement in 2012. "I could have said, 'Mitt, drop to your knees'—he would have dropped to his knees," he said. He said he thought about running again in 2016 but "chickened out," Bloomberg reports. Trump fired back at Romney in a series of tweets. "Just another desperate move by the man who should have easily beaten Barrack Obama," he wrote. "If Trump had said 4 years ago the things he says today about the KKK, Muslims, Mexicans, disabled, I would NOT have accepted his endorsement," he tweeted. Trump supporters on Twitter have started circulating video of Romney praising Trump, according to Bloomberg, which notes that Trump has reversed himself on Romney before.

– Serena Williams has given birth to her first child at a hospital in Palm Beach, Fla., a source close to the tennis champ tells Us. The 35-year-old gave birth to a baby girl at St. Mary's Medical Center on Friday, and both she and her mother are doing well, the source says. E! Online first reported the news, citing a WPBF-25 news producer's tweet that read, "Tennis star SerenaWilliams gives birth toa baby girl weighing 6 pounds, 13 ounces. Mom and baby doing well." Williams and fiance Alexis Ohanian, co-founder of Reddit, announced their engagement in December 2016, and Us notes that Williams accidentally announced her pregnancy in an April Snapchat photo.

– If you're looking for a 1999 Toyota Corolla that's old enough to vote and consent to sex and can run on Nutella, the Houston Chronicle has just the car for you. "You want a car that gets the job done?" reads the Craigslist ad, which was shared on Twitter by Brendan Tokarski. "It's entertaining for all its honesty." It goes on to explain that the car could be driven off a bridge, into a river, and still survive. "The car will outliving you, it will outlive your child," the ad reads. Other amusing notes in the ad include the car's favorite food is spaghetti, its favorite TV show is Alf, and it likes listening to Bush and the Gin Blossoms. "When I ran the CarFax for this car, I got back a single piece of paper that said, 'It's a Corolla. It's fine,'" the ad continues. The ad has since been taken down, but the Mirror notes that plenty of Corolla fans have been tweeting their support.

– Police in Los Angeles say they've "had it" with the "Cold Hand Bandits," a trio of men who have robbed 23 banks since November, NBC News reports. The suspects—described as black men between 25 and 30 years old—walk into a bank with one hand in their pocket and hand a teller a note saying they're armed and threatening violence. "They demand money and claim to be armed while threatening violence if their demands are not met," the LAPD says in a statement. "Law enforcement is greatly concerned that the level of aggression the suspects are displaying is increasing as the bank robberies continue," CBS Los Angeles reports. In the most recent robbery, the suspects jumped over the teller's counter, police say. "This isn't amateur hour," Det. Charles Geiger of the LAPD's Robbery-Homicide Division tells NBC. "The next level is people get tied up, maybe somebody gets hurt." Police are working with the FBI, Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department, Alhambra police, Pasadena police, and Long Beach police to catch the suspects, who are considered armed and dangerous.

– The death toll in the wake of Hurricane Sandy has risen to at least 40, with many of the victims killed by falling trees, the AP reports. More than 6.6 million homes and businesses are without electricity in New York and New Jersey, and the number is expected to rise, the New York Times reports. "We have not seen damage like this in a generation," New York Gov. Andrew Cuomo told NBC News. President Obama has declared major disasters in New Jersey and New York, making them eligible for federal assistance for rebuilding. "All of us have been shocked by the force of mother nature," said the president, who plans to visit New Jersey today. He promised "all available resources" for recovery efforts. "This is going to take some time. It is not going to be easy for these communities to recover." There was no immediate estimate of the losses from the storm, but the scope of the damage—covering more than a half-dozen states—pointed to billions of dollars. Gov. Chris Christie of New Jersey called it "incalculable." The New York region began the daunting process of rebuilding in the aftermath of Sandy, a storm that remade the landscape and rewrote the record books as it left behind a tableau of damage, destruction, and grief. The toll—in lives disrupted or lost and communities washed out—was staggering. A rampaging fire reduced more than 100 houses to ash in Breezy Point, Queens. Explosions and downed power lines left the lower part of Manhattan and 90% of Long Island in the dark. Accidents claimed more than 40 lives in the US and Canada, including 22 in the city. Two boys—an 11-year-old Little League star and a 13-yearold friend—were killed when a 90-foot-tall tree smashed into the family room of a house in North Salem, NY. An off-duty police officer who led seven relatives, including a 15-month-old boy, to safety in the storm drowned when he went to check on the basement.

– Cows belch a lot of methane, and that's a big problem for the planet, the Washington Post reports. According to the EPA, the methane produced by cows and other ruminant animals accounts for 26% of the country's total emissions of the greenhouse gas. And the livestock supply chain alone emits 44% of all human-caused methane on the planet. "Where would human civilization be without them?" asks the Post. Dutch life sciences and materials company DSM has been working on a solution for a while, and a new study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences shows that it can reduce methane emissions from high-producing dairy cows by 30%. The powder, called 3-nitrooxypropanol (3NOP), can be added to cow feed and reduce the amount of methane emitted by the animals by 30%, Scientific American reports. The powder inhibits an enzyme used by the cow's gut to digest grass and other fiber-rich foods. Without that enzyme producing methane, the cow has more energy that can be used for growth and milk production. “In our case, that energy didn’t go to milk production, but the cows actually gained more body weight, basically the energy was directed towards body weight gain,” Penn State researcher Alexander Hristov tells Scientific American. In addition to reducing methane emissions, 3NOP also helped the cows gain weight and made their feed more efficient. Researchers say this is the first time a methane inhibitor has had a "meaningful effect while being safe for cows’ health and the environment." (Scientists have found a way to make cow farts disappear.)

– Steve Bannon came to Alabama to stump for Roy Moore, but he spent more time attacking national Republicans—including Mitt Romney—than Doug Jones, his Democratic opponent in next week's Senate election. Romney and his five sons topped Bannon's hit list, which also included Sen. Jeff Flake and Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell, NBC News reports. In a particularly nasty and personal attack, Bannon, the Breitbart News chairman and former presidential strategist, went after Romney's children and Mormon faith, slamming the former GOP presidential nominee for not serving in the Vietnam War and his sons for not putting on the uniform. "You hid behind your religion. You went to France to be a missionary while men were dying in Vietnam. Do not talk about honor and integrity," Bannon said. "We have 7,000 dead and 52,000 casualties, and where were the Romneys during those wars?" he continued. Bannon is trying to keep Romney, one of the GOP's most prominent Trump critics, from launching a bid for a Utah Senate seat, the Christian Science Monitor reports. At a Moore rally in Fairhope, Alabama, on Tuesday night, Bannon was infuriated by Romney's tweet on Monday in which he wrote that Moore would be a stain on the GOP and on the nation. "Roy Moore in the US Senate would be an embarrassment to the Republican Party and to the nation," Romney wrote. "No vote, no majority is worth losing our honor, our integrity." Bannon's attack on Romney probably won't have any effect on the Alabama race, but it may come back to bite Bannon's preferred candidate in Utah. A recent poll showed that three of four Utahans want Hatch to retire, and Romney would destroy him if the 2018 election were held today. Regardless of whether Hatch or Romney run, now Bannon's endorsement will be toxic to any candidate inUtah. The state is 63% Mormon, and presumably those voters—who aren't big Trump fans to begin with—do not see Romney's missionary work as a flimsy excuse to avoid Vietnam service."

– The House voted today to bar President Obama's executive actions on immigration from having any "legal effect," the AP reports. The vote was 219-197, with three Democrats and seven Republicans voting against. The measure is "largely symbolic," the Washington Post notes, as the Senate is under Democratic control until January and Obama has threatened to veto it. Still, the Hill notes that it's the first step in a two-part strategy: The second step will be a vote on a spending bill next week that would fund the government for a year, with Homeland Security funding only in place for a few months. The goal is to avoid a government shutdown on Dec. 11, the New York Times reports. Ted Cruz and other conservatives are pushing for that bill to include language that would prevent Homeland Security from implementing Obama's plan, and John Boehner is urging Democrats to support it, Politico reports. "If they have 218, if it isn't too brutal, the president will have to make a decision," Boehner says. "But we don't have to vote against our interest if they have the 218. We're willing to compromise if they want to do that to one of us." Nancy Pelosi, meanwhile, called the bill "cold-hearted" and said it would "throw thousands upon thousands of American children into foster care," the Los Angeles Times notes. "Millions of hardworking, law-abiding families will be able to celebrate the holidays with a renewed hope in the future," she said. "With this bill, Republicans would tear apart millions of families."

– More than 20 unpublished poems by Pablo Neruda have been found among the Nobel Prize-winning poet's papers in Chile, reports the Guardian. The poems, which range from love to everyday objects, are of "extraordinary quality" and are full of "the imaginative power, the overflowing expressive fullness, and the same gift, the erotic or loving passion" as Neruda's best works, says academic Pere Gimferrer, who has confirmed that the poems are his. They will be published in Latin America and Spain in 2014 and 2015. Neruda died in 1973 at the age of 69, 12 days after the military coup that put Augusto Pinochet in power in Chile. The official cause of death is cancer, but his driver has said that Pinochet injected poison into the poet's stomach as he was bedridden at a clinic in Santiago, the AP reports. Pinochet feared that if Neruda went into exile from Chile, as was his plan, he would become a voice of opposition to the regime. The poet's body was exhumed in 2013, but forensic tests found no suspicious chemical agents in his bones. Now a judge has ordered new tests for substances that were not found during the first round of investigations, and they will be carried out in Spain and other countries.

– President Trump arrives in Germany on Friday for the G20 summit, but he'll first be in Poland, where the right-wing government of Jaroslaw Kaczynski is brimming with confidence. "We have a new success, Trump’s visit," the head of the governing party said last week, adding that other European countries "envy" the visit, the New York Times reports. The ruling Law and Justice Party, which has been in power since 2015, is trying to limit the ability of courts to operate independently, crack down on dissenting media, and pursue conservative policies on abortion and gay rights, USA Today reports. Poland's leadership sees Trump's visit as a diplomatic coup because it indicates tacit approval for its policies, and comes before he meets with leaders of staunch US allies Britain, France, and Germany. "Unless they do something wrong, pretty much every American president is very popular and will get a warm welcome," Aleks Szczerbiak, an expert on Polish politics at the University of Sussex, tells the BBC. Trump will give Poland the prestige of a visit that will bolster the government against critics that say it has isolated itself, and he will receive a warm reception in a major European country run by a government that shares some of his ideology. The visit will "celebrate Poland's emergence as a European power," National Security Adviser HR McMaster says.

– "Now I can say that I still haven't had an abortion, but I wish I had," Lena Dunham said on her podcast last week. The Girls creator and star was discussing women's stories of abortion when she was asked to share her own. "I wanted to make it really clear to her that as much as I was going out and fighting for other women's options, I myself had never had an Abortion," Dunham said. "And I realized then that even I was carrying within myself stigma around this issue. Even I, the woman who cares as much about a woman's right to choose, felt it was important that people know that I was unblemished in this department." She added: "It was an important moment for me then to realize that I had internalized some of what society was throwing at us. And I had to put it in the garbage.” Dunham went on to say that while she still feels conflicted about terminating a pregnancy, "many women feel conflicted" about doing so and that it's "insulting to women who have actually gone through it." Reaction to her comments has been swift: "Abortion is a medical procedure. Like most medical procedures, it is unpleasant, time-consuming, and costly. There's blood involved, and doctors prodding your vagina, and cramping, and waiting room muzak infused with the smell of anxiety hormones and hospital sanitizer. For many women, there's also a moral component to abortion," writes Amanda Marcotte at the Daily Beast. "Dunham's comments are free red meat for the sort of troll who believes pro-choice feminists spend their days praying to their lord and savior Margaret Sanger that they'll get accidentally impregnated so they can have one of those abortions the gals at the nail salon can't stop gabbing about. It's not compassionate; it's bizarre, a more obnoxious version of telling a person who has had their appendix removed that you, too, wish you could have your appendix taken out." "Women don't need Lena Dunham to 'normalize' abortion by having one. The only thing comments like Dunham's serve to normalize is that tucked away in urban centers live a tribe of feminist caricatures, 30-year-old millennial feminists that have the empathy and self-awareness of petulant teens," writes Jessica Valenti at Time. "Treating abortion like a leisurely activity that Lena Dunham can personally help normalize represents a cartoonish version of clueless urban liberalism we'd be well-served to rid ourselves of."

– Between 2007 and 2017, 13 babies were born in France without arms, forearms, or hands, the Guardian reports. According to FranceInfo, that's 58 times the normal number of babies born with such malformations in the village of Druillat in the Ain department. And while the number of cases in Ain is within the national average, some scientists believe it could be linked to the use of pesticides in the agricultural industry. "There is something, some product, that is cutting the limbs at the time the embryo is developing," Emmanuelle Amar, an epidemiologist and director of the group Remera, tells the Guardian. "We must search for it. We must ask ourselves, what could be capable of halting this development and find it." Amar says pesticides are the most likely cause of the birth defects, but French health officials say there's no evidence of a common cause and won't be investigating further. "I am outraged that no investigation has yet been launched," Céline Figueiredo, whose 4-year-old son was born in Ain without a hand, tells FranceInfo. "They must try to give us answers rather than cover up the cases." A rep for Remera says "no environmental factors—pesticides, for example—could be questioned." The rep adds, "We are definitely facing an excess of cases. We have the scientific and moral obligation to go further." A public health authority has also investigated the cases and found no common exposure among the mothers. "The only thing they have in common is that they all live in a very rural area," Amar says.

– Lady Gaga made a surprise appearance at the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival on Saturday and debuted a new song, Us Weekly reports. "I've been so excited for this next part of the show because I've been trying to keep it a secret for so long," Gaga told the crowd. She played "The Cure" for the first time about halfway through her 90-minute set. Later, as fans were leaving, Gaga announced that the song was out on iTunes.

– "If you've heard the phrase, 'he got off on a technicality,' this is exactly that situation," says Mesa County DA Dan Rubinstein of 46-year-old Michael McFadden, who was released from prison Tuesday after being convicted in 2015 of sexually assaulting six children. McFadden appealed his 2015 conviction, claiming that delays in jury selection violated his right to a speedy trial. In June 2017, the Colorado Court of Appeals ruled in his favor and threw out his conviction. The Colorado Supreme Court refused to hear the appeal on Feb. 12, meaning the lower courts ruling would stand. Rubinstein says McFadden's defense team submitted a jury questionnaire that mentioned his prior sexual offenses, but the judge didn't read it until halfway through jury selection. When he did, he decided McFadden couldn't get a fair trial and granted him a continuance. The defense objected and asserted their right to the statutory speedy trial, and the court of appeals ruled that the defense was, in part, at fault for the jury hearing this information. "The error here was that he shouldn't have been tried longer than six months from the last time he waived speedy trial," Rubinstein tells KKCO. "There was no remedy for that and therefore there is no ability to retry him." The district attorney says he has been in touch with state lawmakers to change the statute.

– Robert Mueller's indictment of 13 Russian nationals and three Russian organizations for alleged election interference continues to reverberate on the Sunday talk shows, with former Director of National Intelligence James Clapper telling CNN's State of the Union that there are "more shoes to drop" in the Mueller investigation. "Not addressed here is potential involvement of the Russian government, which we certainly saw through intelligence channels that the Russiangovernment was masterminding this," Clapper said, per the Hill. "What was the big revelation to me was never before have we seen an effort like this mounted by the Russians,” Clapper said. "The results of the election were not impacted. The Trump campaign did nothing wrong - no collusion!" Elsewhere on your Sunday dial: Rush Limbaugh on the Mueller indictments: "I would be very careful if I was President Trump here. The danger for the president is [that] it would be ... seductive for him to embrace this [and] say, 'See, I've been vindicated.'" Limbaugh says the Mueller probe is "about setting Trump up for impeachment if the Democrats win in 2018. He's the target." "The reason Hillary [Clinton] isn't charged is because that would mean [Barack] Obama would have to be exposed as participating in the scheme, too." "Now that Adam Schiff is starting to blame President Obama for Russian meddling in the election, he is probably doing so as yet another excuse that the Democrats, lead by their fearless leader, Crooked Hillary Clinton, lost the 2016 election. But wasn’t I a great candidate?" Trump on the Parkland shooting: "Very sad that the FBI missed all of the many signals sent out by the Florida school shooter. This is not acceptable. They are spending too much time trying to prove Russian collusion with the Trump campaign - there is no collusion. Get back to the basics and make us all proud!"

– Anderson Cooper is coming to daytime TV. The CNN anchor will host a one-hour daily daytime show that will be nationally syndicated in the fall of 2011, reports Bloomberg. Cooper, 43, will also continue to anchor his 10pm show on CNN. The new show will cover social issues, trends, events, pop culture, celebrities, and human interest news, according to a statement from CNN. Cooper will hold town hall meetings and conduct undercover investigations for the show. “Over the course of the past few years, I’ve had the opportunity to work on a number of daytime programs,” Cooper said in the statement. "It’s fun and interesting to work in daytime television. The format is unique and you can really go in-depth on a wide range of fascinating and compelling stories. With this new program I hope to relay important information and relate to people and the audience in a completely different way."

– Marco Rubio took to the airwaves today to push for immigration reform, appearing on a record-setting seven networks to do so, Politico reports. "What we have in place today, the status quo, is horrible for America," he said on Meet the Press. "The only people benefiting from thestatus quo in immigration today are the people trafficking human beings across the border, and the people who are hiring illegal labor for cheap purposes.” Elsewhere on your Sunday dial, as per Politico: On Jay-Z's Cuba trip: "Jay-Z needs to get informed. One of his heroes is Che Guevara. Che GUEvara was a racist that wrote extensively about the superiority of white Europeans over people of African descent, so he should inform himself on the guy that he’s propping up." On the Gang of Eight's immigration plan: "What North Korea has is not a government. What North Korea is is a criminal syndicate." On gun control: "Guns are what people are using. But violence is our problem. I think this is a missed opportunity to have an honest and open conversation in this country about why these horrifying things are happening, not simply what they're using to carry this out, but why are people doing this to begin with?" On the Manchin-Toomey deal: "The bigger point is the travel policies. The travel policies need to be tightened because they are being abused. These are tourist trips, and they are—what they’re doing is providing hard currency and funding so that a tyrannical regime can maintain its grip on the island of Cuba, and I think that's wrong."

– Glassdoor.com has sifted through 80,000 interview questions shared by job seekers providing us with some of the most odd and difficult questions asked by top-flight employers this year. Think you're smart enough to work at Goldman Sachs or Google? Check out the questions to see just how far out the box employers are expecting their interviewees to think.

– New Zealand's Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern has given birth to her first child, a girl weighing 7.3 pounds, making her only the second elected leader in modern history to give birth while in office, the BBC reports. The 37-year-old was admitted to a hospital in the city of Auckland early Thursday morning, four days after her due date. "I'm sure we're going through all of the emotions new parents go through, but at the same time feeling so grateful for all the kindness and best wishes from so many people. Thank you," she said in a statement. She announced in January that she and partner Clarke Gayford were expecting a baby. The BBC notes that while she will be taking six weeks of maternity leave, she will still be consulted on major issues and will continue to read cabinet papers. Former New Zealand Prime Minister Helen Clark was among the first to congratulate the new prime minister, tweeting, "New Zealand, remain your understated self."

– A photo of Ulysses S. Grant at City Point, Virginia, that's made famous by the Library of Congress is actually "a montage or composite of several images," the library explains in a press release. The head comes from a 1864 portrait of Grant, the horse from a photo of Maj. Gen. Alexander McDowell McCook, and the setting from an 1864 photo of Confederate prisoners who were captured at Fisher's Hill, Va. "The composite photograph tells us something," the press release says. "It admonishes us to not believe everything we see and to investigate what we aren't quite sure is true. While the photo is a disappointment to Civil War buffs, it further creates skepticism in us when seeing photographs produced today. What is real, and what has been created to appear real?" NPR reports that the photo was pieced together for effect more than 100 years ago. It was brought to the library's attention by a researcher in 2007, and history detectives at the library spent years unraveling the mystery. The photo was created by Levin Corbin Handy, a photographer who was the nephew of Civil War photographer Mathew Brady. After Brady died in 1896, Handy inherited his negatives and began "producing new prints from the negatives and licensing images to various publications," according to NPR. He also created "patriotic" images, blurring the line between fact and fiction for those veterans who wanted mementos of their war experience, much like soldiers do today, in fact."

– A Roman shipwreck discovered off the coast of northeastern Italy is "a jewel of the Old World," archaeologists say—but it's not gold or silver it's carrying. Instead, it's thousands of jars of garum, a fish sauce so popular among Romans that it was known as ketchup, Discovery reports. The ship, which was found at a depth of 650 feet, dates to the first century AD and was carrying between 2,000 and 3,000 jars of the stuff, which is made from fermented fish guts and blood. It was mass-produced in Spain and Portugal. "We knew [the shipwreck] was an important find but had no real idea until now," the archaeologist who led the team tells the AP. He says the ship likely left the port of Cadiz in Spain—an important hub for the fish sauce industry at the time—and sailed along the coast between Italy, Spain, and Portugal, "possibly during difficult weather." It's one of just five "deep sea" Roman vessels ever found in the Mediterranean, and the first one to be found off of Liguria, where the wreck was found, researchers tell the Local. They spent two years searching for the ship and were alerted to its location by a local fisherman two years ago, but it wasn't until they used a remote-operated vehicle to retrieve one of the jars that they realized the ship had been carrying garum. The jars were corroded by seawater or removed by curious octopus, but traces of the sauce remain inside, Discovery notes. (In other shipwreck news, archaeologists say they've found the world's oldest shipwreck.)

– In March, 10% of the electricity generated in the US came from wind and solar power, according to the US Energy Information Administration.
